items.q: What is the key structural difference between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells?
items.correct: Eukaryotes have a membrane-bound nucleus and membrane-bound organelles; prokaryotes do not.
items.misconception: Prokaryotes have no DNA — that's why they have no nucleus.
items.random_wrong: Prokaryotes are always smaller than 1 micrometre and have no cytoplasm.
items.why: Students see 'no nucleus' and think 'no DNA', missing that prokaryotes still have DNA — it's just free in the cytoplasm.

items.q: What does a gene code for?
items.correct: Typically a polypeptide (protein) — through transcription to mRNA and translation by ribosomes.
items.misconception: A gene codes for a specific physical feature like eye colour directly.
items.random_wrong: Each gene codes for an organ or body part as a whole.
items.why: Students learn 'gene for eye colour' shorthand, then think genes directly produce traits rather than producing proteins that contribute to traits.

items.q: How do vaccines protect you from a disease?
items.correct: They expose your immune system to a harmless form of the pathogen so memory cells form, allowing rapid response if exposed later.
items.misconception: Vaccines kill the pathogen if it ever enters the body.
items.random_wrong: Vaccines provide antibodies for life by adding them directly to the blood.
items.why: Students think vaccines actively fight pathogens themselves, rather than priming the immune system to fight.

items.q: Can antibiotics cure a cold or the flu?
items.correct: No — colds and flu are caused by viruses, and antibiotics only kill bacteria.
items.misconception: Antibiotics cure viral illnesses like colds and flu.
items.random_wrong: Antibiotics work by killing the patient's own infected cells.
items.why: Patients expect antibiotics for any illness, but viruses lack the structures antibiotics target, so taking them needlessly drives resistance.

items.q: Does a vaccine give you the disease so your body can fight it off?
items.correct: No — a vaccine contains a dead, weakened or partial pathogen, so memory cells form without causing the illness.
items.misconception: A vaccine gives you the real disease in a mild form so you recover.
items.random_wrong: A vaccine adds ready-made antibodies that protect you for life.
items.why: Students fear vaccines cause illness, but they safely trigger the immune memory needed for a fast response to a real future infection.
The answer examiners want is: A protist. “A bacterium” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What do lymphocytes produce to lock onto pathogens?
items.why: It's Antibodies. “Antigens” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A patient has 5 different white blood cells working: 2 phagocytes and 3 lymphocytes. If each lymphocyte makes 4 antibodies per minute, how many antibodies are made in 1 minute?
items.why: Exact answer: 12. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
